problem_text: Determine whether integral_0^infinity x e^(-x) dx converges, and if so find its value.

content.label: Improper: infinite upper limit
content.applies_when: the upper limit is infinite, so the integral is defined as a limit of proper integrals.

content.meaning: integral u dv = u v - integral v du; choose u = x, dv = e^(-x) dx, so du = dx, v = -e^(-x).

content.label: Antiderivative of x e^(-x) is -x e^(-x) - e^(-x)
rewrite integral_0^infinity x e^(-x) dx = lim_{t->infinity} integral_0^t x e^(-x) dx
content.action: rewrite integral_0^infinity x e^(-x) dx = lim_{t->infinity} integral_0^t x e^(-x) dx
content.purpose: convert the improper integral into a limit of proper integrals

content.label: apply integration by parts with u=x, dv=e^(-x)dx to get the antiderivative -x e^(-x) - e^(-x)
content.action: apply integration by parts with u=x, dv=e^(-x)dx to get the antiderivative -x e^(-x) - e^(-x)
content.purpose: find an antiderivative before evaluating the limit

content.label: evaluate [ -x e^(-x) - e^(-x) ]_0^t = 1 - t e^(-t) - e^(-t); as t->infinity both t e^(-t) and e^(-t) -> 0, so the limit is 1
content.action: evaluate [ -x e^(-x) - e^(-x) ]_0^t = 1 - t e^(-t) - e^(-t); as t->infinity both t e^(-t) and e^(-t) -> 0, so the limit is 1
content.purpose: evaluate the proper integral and resolve the limit to the value 1
